questions.question: What is the critical difference between creative community groups and group therapy?

options.text: There is no difference—all groups are therapy
Creative community groups are peer-led for social connection + creative engagement; Group therapy is led by licensed therapists for clinical treatment
Creative community groups are more effective than group therapy
Only professional artists can lead creative community groups

questions.explanation: Creative community groups are peer-led or self-organized for social connection and creative engagement (no clinical training required), appropriate for general wellbeing. Group therapy/group art therapy is led by licensed professionals for clinical treatment (trauma, severe mental illness, addiction, etc.) with assessment, treatment planning, and clinical supervision. If you need clinical support, seek group therapy, not a peer creative group.

questions.question: Which group creative format has the lowest pressure for interaction and sharing?

options.text: Collaborative projects where everyone works together on one shared piece
Parallel work (side-by-side) where everyone works on individual projects with minimal interaction and optional sharing
Show-and-tell critique circles with required feedback
All group formats require the same level of interaction

questions.explanation: Parallel work (side-by-side format) is lowest-pressure: Group gathers to work on individual projects with minimal talking during work time, optional check-in/checkout, optional shares at end. Best for introverts or people who want accountability without pressure to collaborate or share deeply. You're together but not required to interact heavily.

questions.question: What are essential group norms to establish upfront for a safe, supportive creative community?

options.text: Everyone must share everything, critique is mandatory, only experts allowed
Confidentiality ('what's shared stays here'), opt-out allowed, no unsolicited critique, all skill levels welcome, process over product
No rules needed—groups should be completely unstructured
Competitive evaluation to motivate everyone to improve

questions.explanation: Safe creative groups establish explicit norms: confidentiality (personal shares stay in group), opt-out (can pass on sharing without explanation), no unsolicited critique (feedback only when requested), all skill levels welcome (no competition), process over product (value showing up, not perfection). These norms create supportive culture vs. competitive or unsafe dynamics.

questions.question: What is 'witnessing without fixing' and why does it matter in creative groups?

options.text: Ignoring what people share because it doesn't matter
Hearing and acknowledging what someone shares ('Thank you for sharing,' 'I hear you') without trying to fix, advise, or solve—people need to be heard, not fixed
Always offering solutions and advice when someone shares
Critiquing everyone's work to help them improve

questions.explanation: Witnessing without fixing means hearing and acknowledging vulnerable shares ('Thank you for sharing that,' 'I hear you,' 'That sounds hard') without offering unsolicited advice or solutions. Creative expression often surfaces emotions—people need to be heard and validated, not fixed. Advice can feel invalidating. Just witness unless they directly ask for problem-solving.

questions.question: When should you leave a creative group or seek group therapy instead?

options.text: After the first meeting if it's not immediately perfect

If group feels competitive/unsafe, consistently triggers trauma responses, or lacks boundaries—OR if you need clinical support (trauma processing, severe mental illness) requiring licensed group therapy
Never leave any group—commitment is everything
Leave whenever you feel any discomfort at all

questions.explanation: Leave peer creative groups if: they feel competitive/judgmental/unsafe, drain rather than energize you, lack boundaries (clinical oversharing without facilitation), violate norms. Seek licensed group therapy instead if you need clinical support for trauma, severe mental illness, addiction, eating disorders—peer groups aren't appropriate for clinical conditions. Creative community should support wellbeing, not undermine it.

title: Group Creative Practices and Community Building - Quiz
